task: Design a fixed-size database connection pool (say 20 connections) shared by hundreds of request-handling threads. Threads borrow a connection, run queries, and return it. Describe the data structures, how a thread acquires and releases a connection, how waiting is handled when the pool is exhausted, and how broken connections are detected and replaced. Provide enough detail to implement correctly.
constraints: Specify the acquire/release synchronization and how a thread blocks (with a timeout) when no connection is free, avoiding the lost-wakeup race between a returner and a waiter
Guarantee a connection is never handed to two threads at once and is always returned even when the borrowing code throws (leak prevention via a finally/RAII handoff)
Address detecting and discarding a connection that died while idle (validation/health check) so a broken connection is not handed out, while keeping the pool size invariant
Address fair queuing vs starvation of waiters and what happens to waiters during pool shutdown

task: An engineer wrote a lazy singleton initializer using double-checked locking: check if the instance field is null, if so take a lock, check again, then construct and assign the instance. It is used to build an expensive shared configuration object read by many threads. Critique the implementation for correctness on a modern multicore machine and specify how to make lazy initialization correct and efficient.
constraints: Identify the memory-visibility/reordering hazard: without proper memory ordering a thread can observe a non-null reference to a partially constructed object, and explain the role of volatile/atomic acquire-release or a memory barrier
Address the case where the expensive constructor throws or blocks, so other threads do not deadlock or repeatedly retry construction
Offer a clearly-correct alternative (e.g. initialization-on-demand holder / std::call_once / a language-provided lazy) and state its guarantees
Address whether the constructed object is itself safe to publish and read concurrently afterward (immutability or further synchronization)

task: A high-throughput service maintains request counters (total requests, errors, bytes) that are incremented on every request by many threads and read periodically by a metrics exporter. The current code uses a single mutex-protected struct of counters, and profiling shows lock contention is now the bottleneck. Redesign the counters to scale, and describe how the exporter reads a consistent snapshot.
constraints: Specify a contention-reducing design (per-thread/striped/sharded counters or atomics) and explain why a single lock or even a single atomic per counter becomes a scalability bottleneck under heavy contention (cache-line ping-pong / false sharing)
Address how the exporter aggregates shards into a total and the fact that a read across many shards is not a globally atomic instant, and whether that skew is acceptable
Address overflow/wraparound of counters and monotonicity expected by the metrics backend
Address correctness if threads are created/destroyed dynamically so per-thread counters are not lost when a thread exits

task: Design the money-transfer operation for an in-memory ledger where each account has its own lock and a transfer must atomically debit one account and credit another. Under load, the service occasionally hangs. Diagnose the likely cause and specify a transfer implementation that is both correct and deadlock-free. Provide implementation-level detail.
constraints: Identify the lock-ordering deadlock when transfer(A,B) and transfer(B,A) run concurrently each grabbing one lock, and prescribe a global lock-ordering rule (e.g. by account id) or lock-free/try-lock-with-backoff approach
Guarantee the debit and credit are atomic together so no observer or crash leaves money created or destroyed, including the self-transfer (A==B) edge case
Address overdraft: the balance check and debit must be consistent under concurrency so an account cannot go negative via two concurrent withdrawals
Address fairness/liveness so the chosen scheme does not merely trade deadlock for livelock or starvation

task: A server holds an immutable configuration object read on every request by many threads. Occasionally an admin pushes a new config that must replace the old one atomically, with zero downtime and no locking on the read path. Design the hot-reload mechanism and describe exactly what readers and the writer do.
constraints: Specify a copy-on-write / atomic-pointer-swap publication so readers never see a partially updated config and the read path takes no lock

Address the memory-reclamation hazard: an old config may still be in use by an in-flight reader when it is swapped out, so specify safe reclamation (reference counting, epoch/RCU, or GC reliance) to avoid use-after-free
Guarantee a reader that grabs the config at the start of a request uses one consistent snapshot for that whole request rather than mixing old and new mid-request
Address concurrent writers / rapid successive reloads so updates are serialized and none is lost or applied out of order

task: Review an authentication service that issues and verifies JWTs. The verification code reads the token header to determine the algorithm, then verifies the signature accordingly; tokens are signed with RS256 using the service's RSA key pair. Identify the security flaws in this verification approach and specify how tokens must be validated. Provide implementation-level guidance.
constraints: Identify the algorithm-confusion attacks: trusting the header's alg lets an attacker set alg=none or swap RS256 for HS256 and sign with the public key as the HMAC secret; require the verifier to pin the expected algorithm and key
Require validating standard claims (exp, nbf, iss, aud) and rejecting tokens that omit them, not just checking the signature
Address token revocation/expiry and the impossibility of revoking a stateless JWT before expiry, plus a short-lived-access-token + refresh strategy
Address key management/rotation (kid handling without trusting attacker-controlled key URLs) and clock-skew tolerance on exp/nbf

task: Review a reporting feature that builds SQL queries from user-supplied filters: the user picks a column to sort by, a sort direction, a page size, and a free-text search term, and the backend assembles a query string from these. The team currently escapes quotes in the search term. Identify the injection risks and specify a robust, complete approach to building these queries safely.
constraints: Explain why manual quote-escaping is insufficient and require parameterized queries / prepared statements for all value interpolation, with concrete handling of the search term
Address the parts that cannot be parameterized (column name for ORDER BY, sort direction) and require all-listing them against a fixed set rather than escaping
Address LIKE-wildcard and numeric/limit injection (page size, offset) including unbounded or negative values causing denial of service
Address defense in depth: least-privilege DB account, and not leaking SQL errors or stack traces to the client

task: Design a file-upload feature where users upload profile images that are later served back to other users. Specify how uploads are received, validated, stored, and served. Security is the priority. Provide enough detail to implement.
constraints: Address path traversal in the supplied filename (e.g. ../../) and require generating a server-controlled storage name rather than trusting the client filename
Address content validation: do not trust the Content-Type or extension; verify the actual file type, enforce size limits, and prevent decompression bombs / oversized images

Address serving the file safely so an uploaded HTML/SVG/script is not served as active content (correct Content-Type, Content-Disposition, sandboxing or a separate origin/CDN, no execution in the upload directory)
Address authorization on both upload and retrieval so a user cannot overwrite or read another user's files, and storage-key unpredictability

task: Review how a service handles its secrets (database password, third-party API keys, a signing key). Currently they are read from environment variables set at deploy time, and the service logs its full configuration at startup for debugging. Identify the problems and design a sound secrets-handling approach for storage, access, rotation, and exposure. Provide concrete guidance.
constraints: Identify the leakage vectors: secrets in startup config logs, in error reports/stack traces, in crash dumps, and in process listings/child-process environments, and specify how to prevent each
Specify secret storage and delivery (a secrets manager / mounted files with restricted perms) rather than baking them into images or source, and least-privilege access
Address rotation: how a secret can be rotated without downtime and how the service picks up the new value, including overlapping validity for signing keys
Address comparing/handling secrets in code (no logging, constant-time comparison for tokens, scrubbing from memory where feasible) and revocation when a leak is suspected

task: Design the server side of an OAuth2 authorization-code login flow where your web app lets users sign in with a third-party identity provider. Describe the redirect to the provider, the callback handling, and how you establish a session. Security is the priority. Provide implementation-level detail.
constraints: Require an unguessable, per-request state parameter bound to the user's session and verified on callback to prevent CSRF / login-CSRF, and explain the attack it stops
Require exact-match validation of redirect_uri against a pre-registered allow-list to prevent authorization-code interception via open redirect, and use of PKCE
Address exchanging the code server-side (never exposing the client secret), validating the returned ID token (issuer, audience, nonce, signature, expiry), and guarding against code replay/reuse
Address session fixation after login (rotate the session identifier) and secure session cookie attributes (HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite)

task: Review a module that encrypts sensitive fields (e.g. stored access tokens) before writing them to the database. The current code uses AES to encrypt each value with a hardcoded key and a fixed initialization vector, then stores the ciphertext. Identify the cryptographic flaws and specify a correct design for encrypting these fields. Provide implementation-level guidance.
constraints: Identify the fixed-IV / ECB-style determinism flaw (identical plaintexts produce identical ciphertext, leaking equality and enabling other attacks) and require a unique random IV/nonce per encryption, stored alongside the ciphertext
Require authenticated encryption (e.g. AES-GCM or encrypt-then-MAC) so tampering with ciphertext is detected, and explain why unauthenticated CBC is insufficient
Address key management: no hardcoded keys, key storage/derivation, and key rotation with the ability to decrypt old data (key versioning / envelope encryption)
Address nonce-reuse risk specifically for GCM and safe randomness source, plus what is and isn't protected (e.g. searchability vs security tradeoffs)

task: Design a multi-step booking workflow that reserves a flight, charges a card, and books a hotel across three independent services, with no shared transaction. If any step fails, earlier steps must be undone. Specify the orchestration, the compensating actions, and how the system stays consistent under partial failure. Provide implementation-level detail.
constraints: Specify the saga structure (orchestrated or choreographed) with a compensating action per completed step and the order in which compensations run on failure
Address compensations that themselves fail or time out (retry, idempotent compensation, and what to do when an action cannot be cleanly undone, e.g. a non-refundable charge)
Address idempotency and retries for each forward step and each compensation, since the orchestrator may crash and resume and re-issue commands
Address the intermediate inconsistent state visible to users/other systems and how it is bounded, surfaced, or reconciled

task: A read-heavy service caches database rows in a distributed cache (e.g. Redis) in front of the primary database. On a write, the code updates the database row and then deletes the cache key so the next read repopulates it. Under concurrency, users sometimes see stale data indefinitely. Diagnose the consistency problem and design a correct cache-update strategy. Provide implementation-level detail.
constraints: Identify the read-repopulate vs write-invalidate race: a concurrent reader can load the old value and write it back into the cache after the writer's delete, leaving a stale entry until TTL, and specify a mitigation (e.g. delete-after-commit ordering, versioning, or read-through with a short TTL)
Address the failure window between the DB commit and the cache delete (the delete can be lost if the process crashes), and how to make invalidation reliable
Address what happens with multiple concurrent writers to the same key and the resulting ordering of invalidations
Address the choice and tradeoffs of write-through vs write-around vs cache-aside here, and a backstop TTL so staleness is always bounded

task: Design an inventory-decrement operation for a flash sale: many concurrent requests across multiple stateless app servers try to buy from a limited stock count, and you must never sell more units than exist (no overselling), while keeping latency low. Describe the data model and the decrement protocol. Provide implementation-level detail.
constraints: Specify an atomic conditional decrement (e.g. UPDATE ... SET stock = stock - 1 WHERE stock > 0, or an atomic operation in the store) so two concurrent buyers cannot both succeed on the last unit, and explain why read-then-write in app code oversells
Address idempotency/retries: a buyer whose request times out and retries must not be charged or decremented twice for the same intent
Address the hot-key contention on a single popular item (all writes serialize on one row/key) and a scaling approach (sharded stock buckets) plus its reconciliation
Address reservation expiry: stock held by abandoned carts/uncompleted payments must be released without double-releasing or leaking units

task: A distributed system issues short-lived access tokens that expire 5 minutes after issue, with the expiry timestamp embedded in the token. Issuers and verifiers run on different machines. Operations occasionally fail because tokens are seen as expired immediately, or accepted slightly too long. Diagnose the role of time here and design a robust token-expiry and time-handling scheme. Provide implementation-level detail.
constraints: Identify clock skew between issuer and verifier machines as the cause and prescribe bounded skew tolerance (a small leeway window) plus clock synchronization (NTP) expectations
Address the danger of relying on wall-clock time that can jump backward/forward (and why monotonic clocks help for measuring durations but not for absolute expiry across machines)
Address how skew tolerance trades off against security (too much leeway extends a compromised token's life) and how to keep the window tight
Address revocation within the validity window and what happens at the exact boundary (off-by-one on >= vs > the expiry instant)

task: An engineer proposes implementing a 'transfer points between two users' operation that spans two database shards (each user's data lives on a different shard) by opening a transaction on each shard, doing both writes, and committing both. Critique this cross-shard approach and design a correct protocol for the transfer that survives a crash between the two commits. Provide implementation-level detail.
constraints: Explain why two independent local commits are not atomic: a crash after committing shard A but before shard B leaves money lost or duplicated, and naive two-phase commit can block if the coordinator dies
Specify a correct approach (e.g. a transactional outbox + saga, or an idempotent two-step debit-then-credit with a durable intent record) and how it recovers after a crash at each step
Address idempotency so retries during recovery do not double-apply the debit or credit
Address the temporary inconsistency window (points in flight) and how the system avoids exposing created/destroyed value to readers

task: Design the rate-limiting contract for a public API: clients need to understand their current limit, how much they have left, and what to do when throttled. Specify the response behavior and headers for both normal and throttled responses, and the semantics clients must follow. Provide enough detail that client and server teams can implement against it.
constraints: Specify the 429 response and a Retry-After (or reset) signal, and require clients to honor it with backoff rather than retrying immediately, addressing the retry-storm that worsens overload
Specify the meaning of limit/remaining/reset headers precisely, including how 'remaining' behaves under concurrent in-flight requests so clients are not surprised by races at the boundary
Address the window model (fixed vs sliding) and the burst-at-window-boundary problem where a client can send 2x the limit across two adjacent fixed windows
Address distinguishing rate-limit 429 from other 4xx/5xx, idempotency of retried requests, and limits applied per-key vs per-IP vs per-endpoint

task: Design the error-response contract for a JSON REST API used by many client teams. Today every service returns errors in an ad-hoc shape, and clients break when wording changes. Specify a consistent, machine-readable error contract and the rules services must follow, with enough detail to standardize across teams.
constraints: Specify a stable, machine-readable error schema (e.g. a stable error code field clients branch on, plus human-readable message, and correlation id) and require clients branch on the code, not the message text or HTTP status alone
Map error categories to correct HTTP status codes (validation vs auth vs not-found vs conflict vs rate-limit vs server error) and address ambiguous cases like 401 vs 403 and 404-for-authorization

Require not leaking sensitive internals (stack traces, SQL, internal IDs) in errors while still giving clients enough to act, and consistency of the shape even for framework-generated errors (404/500)
Address partial/multi-error responses (field-level validation errors) and how the contract evolves without breaking existing clients (additive fields, new codes are non-breaking)

task: Design a PATCH endpoint for updating a user resource where clients may update any subset of fields, including clearing optional fields to null. Specify the request semantics, the wire format, and how the server applies the update. Provide enough detail to implement and to document for clients.
constraints: Disambiguate 'field omitted (leave unchanged)' from 'field present and set to null (clear it)' from 'field set to a new value', and specify a format (e.g. JSON Merge Patch or explicit semantics) that expresses all three unambiguously

Address concurrent updates / lost updates: two clients patching different fields of the same resource, and require optimistic concurrency (version/ETag/If-Match) so one patch does not silently clobber another
Address partial validation failure (some fields valid, some not) and whether the update is all-or-nothing, plus idempotency of repeated identical PATCHes
Address rejecting unknown/read-only/immutable fields rather than silently ignoring them, and the response body returned

task: Design a bulk endpoint that accepts up to 1000 items in one request to create or update records, for clients importing data. Specify the request and response contract and the processing semantics. Provide enough detail to implement and to document for clients.
constraints: Define the partial-failure semantics precisely: if item 437 fails, what happens to the others (all-or-nothing transaction vs per-item best-effort) and how per-item success/failure is reported back with stable indices/ids
Require idempotency for the whole batch and/or per item (client-supplied keys) so a retried batch after a timeout does not double-create records
Address resource limits: max batch size, total payload size, request timeout vs processing time for large batches, and whether very large imports should be an async job instead

Address ordering and intra-batch duplicates/conflicts (two items in the same batch targeting the same record) and validation error reporting that lets the client fix and resubmit only the failed items

task: Design optimistic-concurrency control for a document-editing API where multiple clients can GET a document, edit it, and PUT it back. Without coordination, a later save silently overwrites an earlier one. Specify the contract (headers, status codes, version model) and the server logic to detect and reject conflicting writes. Provide implementation-level detail.
constraints: Specify the version token mechanism (ETag + If-Match, or a version number) and the exact conflict response (e.g. 412 Precondition Failed / 409) so a stale write is rejected rather than applied
Address the check-and-write race on the server: the version comparison and the write must be atomic (e.g. a conditional UPDATE ... WHERE version = ?) or two simultaneous saves can both pass the check
Address how the version token is generated so it actually changes on every write and is not spoofable/colliding, and how clients recover from a 412 (re-fetch and merge/retry)
Address first-write/creation and deletion races (If-None-Match for create, handling a PUT to a since-deleted resource)

task: Plan an online schema migration on a large, busy production table: you need to add a new non-nullable column with a default and backfill it from existing data, with no downtime and no long table-wide lock. Describe the migration steps, the deploy/rollout sequence, and the safeguards. Provide implementation-level detail.
constraints: Sequence the change to avoid a long blocking lock or a full-table rewrite on a hot table (add nullable/with-safe-default first, backfill in batches, then enforce NOT NULL), and explain why the naive single ALTER is dangerous at scale
Coordinate the schema change with application code deploys so old and new code versions both work during the rollout (expand/contract: code tolerant of the column being absent and then present)
Address the backfill running concurrently with live writes so newly inserted/updated rows are not missed or clobbered by the batch backfill, and the batches do not overwhelm replication/IO
Address rollback/abort safety at each step and verification before the irreversible enforce step

task: A service reads a row, computes a new value in application code, and writes it back (e.g. incrementing a usage counter, or deducting from a balance). Under concurrency, updates are being lost. Explain what is going wrong in terms of transactions and isolation, and specify a correct design including the isolation level or locking strategy. Provide implementation-level detail.
constraints: Identify the lost-update / read-modify-write anomaly and explain why the default READ COMMITTED isolation does not prevent it when two transactions interleave read-then-write

Specify at least one correct fix and its tradeoffs: an atomic in-database update (UPDATE ... SET x = x - ?), SELECT ... FOR UPDATE pessimistic locking, or optimistic version-check with retry, and when each is appropriate
Address the overdraft/invariant case (value must not go negative) so the check and update are enforced atomically, not in app code between two statements
Address deadlocks and retry behavior introduced by locking, and the performance/contention impact of the chosen approach under high concurrency

task: Design a 'register username' (or 'create account by email') operation that must guarantee no two users end up with the same username. The current code checks whether the username exists with a SELECT and, if not, INSERTs it. Under load, duplicates are appearing. Diagnose the problem and specify a correct design. Provide implementation-level detail.
constraints: Identify the check-then-act (TOCTOU) race: two concurrent requests both pass the SELECT and both INSERT, and explain why application-level checking cannot prevent it
Require a database UNIQUE constraint as the source of truth and specify handling the resulting duplicate-key error gracefully (catch and return a clean 409) rather than relying on the pre-check
Address case/normalization (e.g. 'Alice' vs 'alice', Unicode normalization, trailing whitespace) so visually-equal usernames cannot both be created
Address idempotency of retries (a client retrying after a timeout where the first INSERT actually succeeded) and avoiding leaking whether a username/email exists to unauthenticated callers

task: A system uses soft deletes (a deleted_at timestamp) instead of physically removing rows, so data can be restored and audited. The team is now hitting bugs: queries return deleted records, a unique constraint blocks re-creating a previously deleted entity, and storage keeps growing. Design a coherent soft-delete strategy that resolves these. Provide implementation-level detail.
constraints: Address the easy-to-forget requirement that every normal query must filter out soft-deleted rows, and the risk of leaking deleted data through joins, aggregates, or endpoints that miss the filter

Address the unique-constraint conflict: a soft-deleted row still occupies the unique key, blocking re-creation, and specify a fix (partial/filtered unique index on non-deleted rows, or key versioning)
Address foreign-key/referential consistency when a parent is soft-deleted but children are not, and cascade semantics
Address unbounded growth and compliance: an archival/hard-purge policy (and honoring real deletion requests) without breaking audit/restore guarantees

task: A web app uses a primary database for writes and read replicas for reads to scale. After a user updates their profile and is redirected to view it, they sometimes see the old values. Diagnose the cause and design a strategy that gives users a correct view of their own writes while still using replicas for scale. Provide implementation-level detail.
constraints: Identify replication lag and the read-your-own-writes violation when the post-write read is served by a not-yet-caught-up replica
Specify a mitigation (read-from-primary for a window after a write, sticky routing, or wait-for-replica-LSN/version) and its tradeoffs versus simply always reading the primary
Address how long to pin reads to the primary and how to detect/bound replica lag, including what to do when a replica falls far behind or is unavailable
Address the distinction between a user's own writes (must be visible) and other users' data (eventual consistency acceptable), and avoiding the cost of routing all reads to the primary

task: An endpoint that lists 50 orders with their customer and line items has become slow as data grew. Investigation shows it issues one query for the orders, then for each order a query for its customer and another for its items. Diagnose the performance problem and design a fix, including how to keep it from regressing. Provide implementation-level detail.
constraints: Identify the N+1 query pattern and quantify roughly why it scales poorly (query count grows with result size, dominated by per-query round-trip latency), versus the constant/bounded number of queries after the fix
Specify the fix (batched IN-query / join / eager-load with deduplication) and address correctly reassembling the object graph without duplicating rows from the join fan-out
Address pagination and bounding result size so a single request cannot pull unbounded rows, and the interaction of the join/batch with the page limit
Address how to detect this class of bug going forward (query-count assertions in tests, logging/metrics on queries-per-request) so it does not silently return

task: A multi-tenant analytics pipeline shards event storage by tenant_id. One very large tenant now dominates a single shard, causing that shard to run hot (high latency, near capacity) while others sit idle. Diagnose the problem and design a partitioning strategy that avoids hot partitions while keeping queries efficient. Provide implementation-level detail.
constraints: Identify the hot-partition / skew problem caused by a low-cardinality or skewed partition key (one big tenant maps to one shard) and why simply adding shards does not help if the key stays the same

Specify a re-keying/sub-partitioning approach (composite key, salting/bucketing the hot key, or consistent hashing with virtual nodes) and the tradeoff it imposes on queries that must now scan/fan-out across buckets
Address detecting hotness and rebalancing live data without downtime or data loss, and avoiding moving the hotspot to a new single shard
Address the read path: how range/aggregate queries for one tenant still work efficiently after the key change, and bounded fan-out

task: A long-running service's memory grows steadily over days until it is OOM-killed and restarts, though there is no obvious leak in a short test. Outline how you would diagnose the growth and design the fixes for the most likely structural causes. Provide concrete, implementation-level guidance rather than only 'profile it'.
constraints: Enumerate likely structural causes of unbounded growth (an unbounded in-memory cache/map with no eviction, accumulating in a list/queue that is never drained, never-cleaned per-request state, growing thread/connection counts, or retained references via listeners/closures) and how to confirm each
Specify bounding strategies: size/TTL-capped caches with eviction, backpressure on queues, and explicit lifecycle/cleanup of per-key state, with attention to the keys-never-removed case
Address the diagnosis method (heap snapshots/diffs over time, retained-size analysis, allocation tracking) and distinguishing a true leak from legitimately high steady-state usage or fragmentation
Address making the failure safe and observable in the meantime (memory metrics/alerts before OOM, graceful degradation, bounded limits) rather than relying on the OOM-killer restart

task: After every deploy, a service restarts with an empty in-process cache; the first wave of traffic all misses the cache and stampedes the database, briefly taking it down before the cache warms. Design a cold-start/cache-warming and protection strategy so a restart or mass cache expiry does not overload the backing store. Provide implementation-level detail.
constraints: Address the thundering-herd / cache-stampede on cold start and on synchronized mass expiry, and require request coalescing / single-flight so concurrent misses for the same key trigger one backend load, not thousands
Address TTL jitter / staggered expiration so many keys do not expire at the same instant and recreate the stampede later
Specify backend protection during warmup (concurrency limit / load shedding / circuit breaking toward the database) so the warm-up itself cannot take the database down
Address proactive warming and rollout sequencing (pre-populate hot keys, gradual traffic ramp / canary) so not all instances cold-start into full traffic simultaneously

task: An ingestion service reads events from a fast upstream source and writes them to a slower downstream store. Under bursts, memory balloons and the process eventually crashes. Diagnose the cause and design a backpressure strategy so the service degrades gracefully instead of crashing. Provide implementation-level detail.
constraints: Identify the producer-faster-than-consumer problem and the unbounded in-memory buffering that leads to OOM, and require a bounded buffer/queue between the stages

Specify the backpressure mechanism: when the bounded buffer is full, how the upstream is slowed (pause/blocking, flow control, or pulling at the consumer's rate) rather than dropping into unbounded memory
Address the load-shedding vs blocking decision when the source cannot be slowed (e.g. a network firehose): bounded drop with metrics, or spilling to durable storage, and the data-loss tradeoffs
Address recovery after a burst, avoiding a latency death-spiral, and observability (queue depth, drop counts, lag) so saturation is visible before the crash
